title: Homework 3
text:
The relative path that you would specify to get the file is “../MyData.csv”.



The major benefits of using R projects are:

R projects provide a straightforward way to divide your work into multiple contexts. Each with their own:

Working directory
Workspace (environment with stored data/objects)
History
Folder structure & source documents





Git is a version control software.
Github is an online hosting service for Git-based projects.




Data frames match the prefix of any variable names (partial matching) and don’t complain if a column doesn’t exist. Tibbles are more strict: they only ever match variable names exactly and they will generate a warning if the column you are trying to access doesn’t exist. Tibbles are designed to work with functions within the tidyverse where data frames don’t necessarily do this.
Tibble is a modern re-imagining of the data frame, keeping what time has proven to be effective, and throwing out what it has not. Tibbles are data.frames that are lazy and surly: they do less and complain more forcing you to confront problems earlier, typically leading to cleaner, more expressive code.





##original 
library(tidyverse)

── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.5
✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.5.1
✔ ggplot2   4.0.1     ✔ tibble    3.3.0
✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.1
✔ purrr     1.1.0     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

arrange(filter(select(as_tibble(iris), starts_with("Petal"), Species), Petal.Length <
                 1.55), Species)

# A tibble: 37 × 3
   Petal.Length Petal.Width Species
          <dbl>       <dbl> <fct>  
 1          1.4         0.2 setosa 
 2          1.4         0.2 setosa 
 3          1.3         0.2 setosa 
 4          1.5         0.2 setosa 
 5          1.4         0.2 setosa 
 6          1.4         0.3 setosa 
 7          1.5         0.2 setosa 
 8          1.4         0.2 setosa 
 9          1.5         0.1 setosa 
10          1.5         0.2 setosa 
# ℹ 27 more rows

##rewrited
library(tidyverse)

as_tibble(iris) |>
  select(starts_with("Petal"), Species) |>
  filter(Petal.Length < 1.55) |>
  arrange(Species)

# A tibble: 37 × 3
   Petal.Length Petal.Width Species
          <dbl>       <dbl> <fct>  
 1          1.4         0.2 setosa 
 2          1.4         0.2 setosa 
 3          1.3         0.2 setosa 
 4          1.5         0.2 setosa 
 5          1.4         0.2 setosa 
 6          1.4         0.3 setosa 
 7          1.5         0.2 setosa 
 8          1.4         0.2 setosa 
 9          1.5         0.1 setosa 
10          1.5         0.2 setosa 
# ℹ 27 more rows





Long format data is where each row consists of one observation and each column one variable. Wide format data is where we may have more than one observation in a given row. For statistical analysis we prefer long format data as almost all of the plotting, summarizing, and modeling we do in statistics expects data to be in long form.

title: Homework 3
text:
Task 2: Reading Delimited Data
The first data set is called glass.data. This data set is comma delimited. The second data set is called yeast data. This data set is white-space delimited.

library(tidyverse)

# Read data and add column names
glass_cols <- c("Id", "RI", "Na", "Mg", "Al", "Si", "K", "Ca", "Ba", "Fe", "Type_of_glass")
glass_data <- read_csv("https://www4.stat.ncsu.edu/~online/datasets/glass.data", col_names = glass_cols, show_col_types = FALSE)

# Print the tibble
glass_data 

# A tibble: 214 × 11
      Id    RI    Na    Mg    Al    Si     K    Ca    Ba    Fe Type_of_glass
   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>         <dbl>
 1     1  1.52  13.6  4.49  1.1   71.8  0.06  8.75     0  0                1
 2     2  1.52  13.9  3.6   1.36  72.7  0.48  7.83     0  0                1
 3     3  1.52  13.5  3.55  1.54  73.0  0.39  7.78     0  0                1
 4     4  1.52  13.2  3.69  1.29  72.6  0.57  8.22     0  0                1
 5     5  1.52  13.3  3.62  1.24  73.1  0.55  8.07     0  0                1
 6     6  1.52  12.8  3.61  1.62  73.0  0.64  8.07     0  0.26             1
 7     7  1.52  13.3  3.6   1.14  73.1  0.58  8.17     0  0                1
 8     8  1.52  13.2  3.61  1.05  73.2  0.57  8.24     0  0                1
 9     9  1.52  14.0  3.58  1.37  72.1  0.56  8.3      0  0                1
10    10  1.52  13    3.6   1.36  73.0  0.57  8.4      0  0.11             1
# ℹ 204 more rows

# Create a vector of the names in order (1 to 7)
glass_names <- c("building_windows_float_processed", 
                 "building_windows_non_float_processed", 
                 "vehicle_windows_float_processed", 
                 "vehicle_windows_non_float_processed", 
                 "containers", 
                 "tableware", 
                 "headlamps")

glass_data |>
  # Replace the number with the corresponding name from the vector
  mutate(Type_of_glass = glass_names[Type_of_glass]) |>
  # Convert to a factor 
  mutate(Type_of_glass = as.factor(Type_of_glass)) |>
  # Filter the data
  filter(Fe < 0.2, Type_of_glass %in% c("tableware", "headlamps"))

# A tibble: 38 × 11
      Id    RI    Na    Mg    Al    Si     K    Ca    Ba    Fe Type_of_glass
   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <fct>        
 1   177  1.52  14    2.39  1.56  72.4  0     9.57  0        0 tableware    
 2   178  1.52  13.8  2.41  1.19  72.8  0     9.77  0        0 tableware    
 3   179  1.52  14.5  2.24  1.62  72.4  0     9.26  0        0 tableware    
 4   180  1.52  14.1  2.19  1.66  72.7  0     9.32  0        0 tableware    
 5   181  1.51  14.4  1.74  1.54  74.6  0     7.59  0        0 tableware    
 6   182  1.52  15.0  0.78  1.74  72.5  0     9.95  0        0 tableware    
 7   183  1.52  14.2  0     2.09  72.7  0    10.9   0        0 tableware    
 8   184  1.52  14.6  0     0.56  73.5  0    11.2   0        0 tableware    
 9   185  1.51  17.4  0     0.34  75.4  0     6.65  0        0 tableware    
10   186  1.51  13.7  3.2   1.81  72.8  1.76  5.43  1.19     0 headlamps    
# ℹ 28 more rows

# Read data and add column names
yeast_cols <- c("seq_name", "mcg", "gvh", "alm", "mit", "erl", "pox", "vac", "nuc", "class")
yeast_data <- read_table("https://www4.stat.ncsu.edu/~online/datasets/yeast.data", col_names = yeast_cols, show_col_types = FALSE)

# Print the tibble
yeast_data

# A tibble: 1,484 × 10
   seq_name     mcg   gvh   alm   mit   erl   pox   vac   nuc class
   <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>
 1 ADT1_YEAST  0.58  0.61  0.47  0.13   0.5   0    0.48  0.22 MIT  
 2 ADT2_YEAST  0.43  0.67  0.48  0.27   0.5   0    0.53  0.22 MIT  
 3 ADT3_YEAST  0.64  0.62  0.49  0.15   0.5   0    0.53  0.22 MIT  
 4 AAR2_YEAST  0.58  0.44  0.57  0.13   0.5   0    0.54  0.22 NUC  
 5 AATM_YEAST  0.42  0.44  0.48  0.54   0.5   0    0.48  0.22 MIT  
 6 AATC_YEAST  0.51  0.4   0.56  0.17   0.5   0.5  0.49  0.22 CYT  
 7 ABC1_YEAST  0.5   0.54  0.48  0.65   0.5   0    0.53  0.22 MIT  
 8 BAF1_YEAST  0.48  0.45  0.59  0.2    0.5   0    0.58  0.34 NUC  
 9 ABF2_YEAST  0.55  0.5   0.66  0.36   0.5   0    0.49  0.22 MIT  
10 ABP1_YEAST  0.4   0.39  0.6   0.15   0.5   0    0.58  0.3  CYT  
# ℹ 1,474 more rows

# Chain to remove columns and add mean/median for numeric variables
yeast_data |>
  select(-seq_name, -nuc) |>
  group_by(class) |>
  mutate(across(c(mcg, gvh, alm, mit, erl, pox, vac), 
                list(mean = mean, median = median), 
                .names = "{.col}_{.fn}"))

# A tibble: 1,484 × 22

   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>    <dbl>      <dbl>    <dbl>

10  0.4   0.39  0.6   0.15   0.5   0    0.58 CYT      0.481       0.48    0.470

yeast_data

title: Homework 3
section: Task 3: Combining Excel and Delimited Data
text:
Task 3: Combining Excel and Delimited Data
This task examines two sets of data “related to red and white variants of the Portuguese ‘Vinho Verde’ wine.” The first dataset is white wine data that is an excel sheet. The second dataset is a red wine data that is a semi-colon delimited data.

library(readxl)

# Import white wine data 
white_wine <- read_excel("white-wine.xlsx", sheet = 1)
white_wine

# A tibble: 4,898 × 12
   `fixed acidity` `volatile acidity` `citric acid` `residual sugar` chlorides
             <dbl>              <dbl>         <dbl>            <dbl>     <dbl>
 1               7               0.27          0.36             20.7     0.045
 2              63               0.3           0.34              1.6     0.049
 3              81               0.28          0.4               6.9     0.05 
 4              72               0.23          0.32              8.5     0.058
 5              72               0.23          0.32              8.5     0.058
 6              81               0.28          0.4               6.9     0.05 
 7              62               0.32          0.16              7       0.045
 8               7               0.27          0.36             20.7     0.045
 9              63               0.3           0.34              1.6     0.049
10              81               0.22          0.43              1.5     0.044
# ℹ 4,888 more rows
# ℹ 7 more variables: `free sulfur dioxide` <dbl>,
#   `total sulfur dioxide` <dbl>, density <dbl>, pH <dbl>, sulphates <dbl>,
#   alcohol <dbl>, quality <dbl>

# Read column names from the second sheet and overwrite names
wine_names <- read_excel("white-wine.xlsx", sheet = 2) |> 
  #Take the first column and turn into a standard vector of strings
  pull(1)
colnames(white_wine) <- wine_names

# Add column indicating 'white'
white_wine <- white_wine |> mutate(type = "white")
white_wine

# A tibble: 4,898 × 13
   fixed_acidity volatile_acidity citric_acid residual_sugar chlorides
           <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>     <dbl>
 1             7             0.27        0.36           20.7     0.045
 2            63             0.3         0.34            1.6     0.049
 3            81             0.28        0.4             6.9     0.05 
 4            72             0.23        0.32            8.5     0.058
 5            72             0.23        0.32            8.5     0.058
 6            81             0.28        0.4             6.9     0.05 
 7            62             0.32        0.16            7       0.045
 8             7             0.27        0.36           20.7     0.045
 9            63             0.3         0.34            1.6     0.049
10            81             0.22        0.43            1.5     0.044
# ℹ 4,888 more rows
# ℹ 8 more variables: free_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>, total_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>,
#   density <dbl>, pH <dbl>, sulphates <dbl>, alcohol <dbl>, quality <dbl>,
#   type <chr>

# Read semi-colon delimited red wine data, update names, and add type
red_wine <- read_delim("red-wine.csv", delim = ";", col_types = rep("d", 12))
colnames(red_wine) <- wine_names
red_wine <- red_wine |> mutate(type = "red")
red_wine

# A tibble: 1,599 × 13
   fixed_acidity volatile_acidity citric_acid residual_sugar chlorides
           <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>     <dbl>
 1           7.4             0.7         0               1.9     0.076
 2           7.8             0.88        0               2.6     0.098
 3           7.8             0.76        0.04            2.3     0.092
 4          11.2             0.28        0.56            1.9     0.075
 5           7.4             0.7         0               1.9     0.076
 6           7.4             0.66        0               1.8     0.075
 7           7.9             0.6         0.06            1.6     0.069
 8           7.3             0.65        0               1.2     0.065
 9           7.8             0.58        0.02            2       0.073
10           7.5             0.5         0.36            6.1     0.071
# ℹ 1,589 more rows
# ℹ 8 more variables: free_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>, total_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>,
#   density <dbl>, pH <dbl>, sulphates <dbl>, alcohol <dbl>, quality <dbl>,
#   type <chr>

# Combine the data sets
combined_wine <- bind_rows(white_wine, red_wine)
combined_wine

# A tibble: 6,497 × 13
   fixed_acidity volatile_acidity citric_acid residual_sugar chlorides
           <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>          <dbl>     <dbl>
 1             7             0.27        0.36           20.7     0.045
 2            63             0.3         0.34            1.6     0.049
 3            81             0.28        0.4             6.9     0.05 
 4            72             0.23        0.32            8.5     0.058
 5            72             0.23        0.32            8.5     0.058
 6            81             0.28        0.4             6.9     0.05 
 7            62             0.32        0.16            7       0.045
 8             7             0.27        0.36           20.7     0.045
 9            63             0.3         0.34            1.6     0.049
10            81             0.22        0.43            1.5     0.044
# ℹ 6,487 more rows
# ℹ 8 more variables: free_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>, total_sulfur_dioxide <dbl>,
#   density <dbl>, pH <dbl>, sulphates <dbl>, alcohol <dbl>, quality <dbl>,
#   type <chr>

# 6. - 9. Chain to filter, sort, select, and mutate the combined data
combined_wine |>
  #Filter the data to only look at the high-quality wines and reasonable alcohol value
  filter(quality > 6.5, alcohol < 132) |>
  #sort the data from highest quality to lowest
  arrange(desc(quality)) |>
  #select variables containing acid, alcohol, type, and quality
  select(contains("acid"), alcohol, type, quality) |>
  #add mean and sd of the alcohol variable
  group_by(quality) |>
  mutate(mean_alcohol = mean(alcohol), sd_alcohol = sd(alcohol))

# A tibble: 1,206 × 8
# Groups:   quality [3]
   fixed_acidity volatile_acidity citric_acid alcohol type  quality mean_alcohol
           <dbl>            <dbl>       <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>   <dbl>        <dbl>
 1            91             0.27        0.45     104 white       9        122. 
 2            66             0.36        0.29     124 white       9        122. 
 3            74             0.24        0.36     125 white       9        122. 
 4            69             0.36        0.34     127 white       9        122. 
 5            71             0.26        0.49     129 white       9        122. 
 6            62             0.66        0.48     128 white       8         94.1
 7            62             0.66        0.48     128 white       8         94.1
 8            68             0.26        0.42     105 white       8         94.1
 9            67             0.23        0.31     107 white       8         94.1
10            67             0.23        0.31     107 white       8         94.1
# ℹ 1,196 more rows
# ℹ 1 more variable: sd_alcohol <dbl>

title: Homework 3
text:
Task 4: Database Practice

library(DBI)

# Connect to database and look at tables
con <- dbConnect(RSQLite::SQLite(), "Lahman.db")
dbListTables(con)

 [1] "AllstarFull"         "Appearances"         "AwardsManagers"     
 [4] "AwardsPlayers"       "AwardsShareManagers" "AwardsSharePlayers" 
 [7] "Batting"             "BattingPost"         "CollegePlaying"     
[10] "Fielding"            "FieldingOF"          "FieldingOFsplit"    
[13] "FieldingPost"        "HallOfFame"          "HomeGames"          
[16] "LahmanData"          "Managers"            "ManagersHalf"       
[19] "Parks"               "People"              "Pitching"           
[22] "PitchingPost"        "Salaries"            "Schools"            
[25] "SeriesPost"          "Teams"               "TeamsFranchises"    
[28] "TeamsHalf"           "battingLabels"       "fieldingLabels"     
[31] "pitchingLabels"     

# Return 2015 Teams data using tbl() and dplyr
tbl(con, "Teams") |> 
  filter(yearID == 2015) |>
  collect()

# A tibble: 30 × 48
   yearID lgID  teamID franchID divID  Rank     G Ghome     W     L DivWin WCWin
    <int> <chr> <chr>  <chr>    <chr> <int> <int> <int> <int> <int> <chr>  <chr>
 1   2015 NL    ARI    ARI      W         3   162    81    79    83 N      N    
 2   2015 NL    ATL    ATL      E         4   162    81    67    95 N      N    
 3   2015 AL    BAL    BAL      E         3   162    78    81    81 N      N    
 4   2015 AL    BOS    BOS      E         5   162    81    78    84 N      N

# Return Hall of Fame players, year voted into hall of fame, and category
hof_players <- tbl(con, "HallOfFame") |> 
  filter(inducted == 'Y') |> 
  select(playerID, yearID, category)

# Combine with People table to add first and last names
hof_people <- hof_players |>
  left_join(tbl(con, "People") |> 
               select(playerID, nameFirst, nameLast), by = "playerID") |>
  collect()

# Retrieve managers, summarize records, and create win/loss percentage
managers_summary <- tbl(con, "Managers") |>
  select(playerID, G, W, L) |>
  group_by(playerID) |>
  summarize(G_managed = sum(G, na.rm = TRUE),
            Total_W = sum(W, na.rm = TRUE),
            Total_L = sum(L, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  collect() |>
  mutate(win_loss_pct = Total_W / G_managed) |>
  arrange(desc(win_loss_pct))
managers_summary

hof_people |>
  collect() |>
  inner_join(managers_summary, by = "playerID")
